Calvin prizes this verse as a "remarkable eulogy" of love: it is evidence of the most blessed transition possible. Robertson explains the perfect active indicative metabebēkamen—"we have passed over" and still are on the other side—a completed migration from spiritual death to spiritual life, permanent and irreversible. The Greek metabainō is the same verb used in John 7:3 for physical movement from place to place; John applies it to the most decisive relocation in human experience. Wesley draws out the internal logic: "We ourselves could not love our brethren unless we were passed from death to life, that is, born of God." Clarke traces the spatial metaphor: death and life are two distinct territories or kingdoms between which the believer has been transferred by new birth. Barnes calls love "an infallible evidence of true piety," observing that the negative corollary follows immediately: he who does not love his brother remains in death.

voices.html: We have passed (metabebēkamen). Perfect active indicative of metabainō, old compound to pass over from one place to another (Joh 7:3), to migrate, out of death into life. We have already done it while here on earth. Because (hoti)—proof of this transition, not the ground of it.

He commends love to us by a remarkable eulogy, because it is an evidence of a transition from death to life. It hence follows that if we love the brethren we are blessed, but that we are miserable if we hate them. There is no one who does not wish to escape from death.

We know—As if he had said, We ourselves could not love our brethren, unless we were passed from spiritual death to life, that is, born of God. He that loveth not his brother abideth in death—That is, is not born of God.
We know that we have passed from death unto life—Because we love the brethren. The sentiment here is that it is an infallible evidence of true piety that we love the brethren.

15.synthesis: Hatred of a brother is not merely unkindness but murder in character and tendency. Robertson clarifies the rare compound anthrōpoktonos (man-killer), used in John 8:44 of Satan himself—placing the hater in spiritual company with the devil. Calvin uses this verse to stimulate love by showing hatred's true face: "He could have said, 'all who hate their brethren deserve death'; but he takes the very name of what we abhor in order to convict us more sharply." Wesley focuses on the ontological reality: the one who hates "abideth in spiritual death, is void of the life of God"—and further, "every degree of hatred being a degree of the same temper which moved Cain to murder his brother." Clarke observes that hatred has the same inner principle as Cain's act; the outward restraint is circumstantial, not moral. Barnes calls private malice and secret grudge "murderous in its tendency—that which, if it were acted out, would lead him to commit murder, as it did Cain."

voices.html: A murderer (anthrōpoktonos). Old compound from anthrōpos (man) and kteinō (to kill), a man-killer, in N.T. only here and Joh 8:44 (of Satan). Abiding (menousan). Present active feminine predicate participle of menō, "a continuous power and a communicated gift" (Westcott).

To stimulate us still more to love, he shews how detestable before God is hatred. All who hate their brethren are murderers. He could have said they deserve death, but the Apostle takes the very name of what we abhor to convict us more sharply of hatred's true nature.

He, I say, abideth in spiritual death, is void of the life of God. For whosoever hateth his brother—and there is no medium between loving and hating him—is, in God's account, a murderer: every degree of hatred being a degree of the same temper which moved Cain to murder his brother.

He has the spirit of a murderer; he has that which, if it were acted out, would lead him to commit murder, as it did Cain. The private malice, the secret grudge, the envy which is cherished in the heart, is murderous in its tendency.

16.synthesis: The measure of love is Christ's laying down his life—and the implication is that believers are called to the same sacrifice for one another. Robertson notes "love" here with the article (tēn agapēn)—"the thing called love"—as if John means: this at last is the real thing, the standard by which all lesser loves are measured. Both Wesley and Barnes observe that the words "of God" are not in the original Greek; the subject is Christ's love, not divine love in the abstract, making the apostle's point that Christ's sacrifice is the defining paradigm. Clarke sees a direct parallel with John 3:16: "God so loved the world that he gave his only Son"—here the same love displayed in the Son's own death. Calvin notes that true love is shewn not in mere sentiment but in willingness to give what is most precious—life itself. Consensus: Christ's death defines and measures love, and that measure calls believers to costly, sacrificial action toward one another.

voices.html: Know we (egnōkamen). Perfect active indicative, "we have come to know and still know." Love (tēn agapēn)—"The thing called love" (D. Smith). He for us (ekeinos huper hēmōn). Huper here alone in this Epistle, though common in John's Gospel (10:11, 15; 11:50). Laid down his life (tēn psuchēn autou ethēken).

He now shews what true love is; for it would not have been enough to commend it, unless its power is understood. As an instance of perfect love, he sets before us the example of Christ; for he, by not sparing his own life, testified how much he loved us.
The word God is not in the original. It was omitted by the apostle—it is Christ's own laying down of his life that is in view, as the absolute measure and pattern of the love we are to imitate.

The words "of God" are not in the original and should not have been introduced into the translation. The thought is of Christ's sacrifice as the measure and model: by this we have come to know what love is.

17.synthesis: If the supreme measure of love is laying down one's life (v16), a more accessible test follows immediately: sharing material goods with a brother in need. Calvin reasons from the greater to the lesser: "he who refuses to assist his neighbor with his goods, will be much less ready to give himself for him." Robertson notes that bios (livelihood) rather than zōē (life-principle) is the word for goods—worldly substance sufficient only for this age, far less precious than life itself. Wesley captures the moral moment: "whoso hath this world's good... and seeth his brother have need—The very sight of want knocks at the door of the spectator's heart." Clarke makes the test pointed: "if we do not divide our bread with the hungry, we certainly would not lay down our life for him." Barnes adds that "this world's good" is named such because it is of value only this side of the grave. The tradition is unanimous: shutting one's heart against visible need disproves all claim to the sacrificial love of verse 16.

voices.html: He now speaks of the common duties of love, which flow from that chief foundation; for he who refuses to assist his neighbor with his goods will be much less ready to give himself for him. He seems to reason from the greater to the less.

Whoso hath (hos an echē). Indefinite relative clause with modal an. The world's goods (ton bion tou kosmou). Bion, the living or livelihood, not zōē the principle of life—worldly substance, far less valuable than life itself. Beholdeth (theōrei). Present active subjunctive.

But whoso hath this world's good—Worldly substance, far less valuable than life. And seeth his brother have need—The very sight of want knocks at the door of the spectator's heart. And shutteth up his bowels of compassion from him, how dwelleth the love of God in him?

But whoso hath this world's good—Here is a test of this love; if we do not divide our bread with the hungry, we certainly would not lay down our life for him. Whatever love we may pretend to mankind, if we are not charitable and benevolent, we have it not.

18.synthesis: Love expressed only in words or tongue is no love at all—it must manifest in deed and truth. Robertson explains that John does not condemn comforting words in themselves but insists that warm words must be accompanied by warm deeds: "Here is a case where actions do speak louder than mere words." Calvin acknowledges a concession in the first clause—people do make verbal professions—but shows their insufficiency: the Apostle names their dissimulation to rebuke it. Clarke distinguishes three modes: word (intellectual assent that love is right), tongue (verbal profession), and their opposite: deed (concrete action) and truth (genuine sincerity). Wesley is terse: "Not in tongue by empty professions, but in truth." Barnes notes that mere profession is what John has warned against throughout the epistle (1 John 1:6; 2:4–6) and that deed without truth would be mere performance—both action and sincerity are required, not either alone. The call is to integration: the inner truth of love must be visible in outward action.

voices.html: What John means is "not merely by word or by the tongue." He does not condemn kind words which are comforting and cheering, but warm words should be accompanied by warm deeds to make real "in deed and in truth" (en ergō kai alētheia). Here is a case where actions do speak louder than mere words.

There is a concession in this first clause; for we cannot love in tongue only; but as many falsely pretend this, the Apostle, according to what is often done, gives the name of the thing to their dissimulation, though in the second clause he reproves them.

My little children, let us not love in word—in merely allowing the general doctrine of love to God and man to be just and right; Neither in tongue—in making professions of love and of a charitable and human disposition; but in deed and in truth—in real, active benevolence.
Not in word—Only. But in deed—In action: not in tongue by empty professions, but in truth.

19.synthesis: Active, operative love—love in deed and truth (v18)—becomes the ground of assurance before God: by this we know that we are of the truth. Robertson notes the future middle indicative gnōsometha—"at any future emergency, we shall come to know"—suggesting that love provides assurance precisely when assurance is most needed, not only in calm reflection. Calvin reads "truth" as a name for the divine nature: "If we in truth love our neighbors, we have an evidence that we are born of God, who is truth, or that the truth of God dwells in us." Clarke centers on the conscience: love allows the believer to "assure our hearts—be persuaded in our consciences, that we have the truth as it is in Jesus." Wesley adds that real, operative love gives "a farther proof... that we have the true religion of the Lord Jesus." Barnes draws the epistemological point: love-in-deed, not mere profession, is the self-evidence that cannot be manufactured and cannot be doubted.

voices.html: Shall we know (gnōsometha). Future middle indicative of ginōskō, at any future emergency we shall come to know by this that we are of the truth. Before him (emprosthen autou)—in the very presence of God we shall have confident assurance (peisomen tēn kardian hēmōn).

If we, in truth, love our neighbors, we have an evidence that we are born of God, who is truth, or that the truth of God dwells in us. But we must remember that love of the brethren is not the cause of our assurance, but only a sign or evidence of it.

Hereby we know that we are of the truth—That we have the true religion of the Lord Jesus, and shall assure our hearts—be persuaded in our consciences, that we have the truth as it is in Jesus; as no man can impose upon himself by imagining he acts charitably when he does not.

And hereby we know—We have a farther proof by this real, operative love. That we are of the truth—That we have true faith, that we are true children of God. And shall assure our hearts before him—Shall enjoy the assurance of his favour.

20.synthesis: The condemning heart is a serious witness, not easily overridden; yet it is not the final judge. Robertson explains that the construction hoti ean kataginōskē hēmōn hē kardia ("in whatever our heart condemns us") paired with the statement "God is greater than our heart" is not a soft reassurance but a solemn reinforcement: if even our own conscience catches us in hypocrisy, how much more does the omniscient God know it. Calvin warns against those who claim the name of Christian with guilty conscience: "he who is conscious of guilt, and is condemned by his own heart, much less can he escape the judgment of God, who is infinitely more penetrating than our own conscience." Clarke notes the double reality: if our love is feigned, we feel inwardly condemned; and God, who is greater, sees more. Wesley stresses the judicial dimension: God is "an infinitely holier and a more impartial Judge" who "knoweth all things." Barnes notes the reference is primarily to present conduct, not past life.

voices.html: Whereinsoever our heart condemn us (hoti ean kataginōskē hēmōn hē kardia). Kataginōskō means to know something against one, to condemn. Because God is greater than our heart (hoti meizōn estin tēs kardias hēmōn). Ablative after the comparative. And knoweth all things (kai ginōskei panta).

They in vain possess the name and appearance of Christians who have not the testimony of a good conscience. If any one is conscious of guilt and is condemned by his own heart, much less can he escape the judgment of God, who is infinitely more penetrating than our own conscience.

If we have not this testimony, if in anything our heart, our own conscience, condemn us, much more does God, who is greater than our heart—An infinitely holier and a more impartial Judge. And knoweth all things—So that there is no hope of hiding it from him.

The reference here is not so much to our past lives, as to our present conduct and deportment. The object is to show that if our own hearts condemn us, God, who is greater and knows all things, will also condemn us.

21.synthesis: The converse of verse 20 is equally real: when conscience does not condemn, the believer stands before God with confident boldness (parrēsian). Robertson is careful: this is "not a claim to sinlessness, but the consciousness of fellowship in God's presence"—the conscience enlightened by God's Spirit, comparing thoughts, words, and works against the word, and finding agreement. Calvin notes that this confidence does not belong to gross despisers of God who feel nothing, but only to those who have genuinely walked with God under his scrutiny: "This is the balance of God by which he tries men." Clarke draws the practical line: if we practice no deceit and use no mask, "we can appeal to him for our sincerity, and we can come with boldness to the throne of grace." Wesley specifies the instrument: conscience "duly enlightened by the word and Spirit of God" is what pronounces the verdict. Barnes describes the state as an approving conscience that discharges duty faithfully and submits to God's providence without murmuring.

voices.html: If our heart condemn us not (ean hē kardia mē kataginōskē). Condition of third class with ean mē and present active subjunctive. The converse of the preceding, but not a claim to sinlessness—rather the consciousness of fellowship in God's presence. Boldness toward God (parrēsian pros ton theon).

I have already explained that this refers not to the gross despisers of God; for how much soever the reprobate may approve of their own lives, yet as Solomon says, they are inwardly reproved. This balance of God, by which he tries men, is such that no one will be found free from guilt, except he is reconciled to God.

If our heart condemn us not—If we be conscious to ourselves of our own sincerity, that we practise not deceit and use no mask, then have we confidence toward God—we can appeal to him for our sincerity, and we can come with boldness to the throne of grace.

If our conscience, duly enlightened by the word and Spirit of God, and comparing all our thoughts, words, and works with that word, pronounce that they agree therewith, then have we confidence toward God—our consciousness of his favour continues and increases.

22.synthesis: Prayer flows freely from the conscience that does not condemn, because such a conscience belongs to the one who keeps God's commandments and does what pleases him. Robertson notes the indefinite relative clause ho ean aitōmen—"whatsoever we ask"—but observes that the unstated limitation is complete fellowship with God, which means complete surrender of will to the Father's will; Robertson compares Jesus' own Gethsemane prayer as the paradigm. Calvin connects purity of worship to efficacy of prayer: "none can really pray to God but those who with a pure heart fear and rightly worship him. The latter follows from the former." Clarke reduces the commandments to their essence: love God and love neighbor. Barnes cites the multiple instances where Jesus taught the same principle (Matt 7:7; Mark 11:24; John 14:12–13; 16:23)—prayer within God's will and character is prayer that God delights to answer. The promise is real, but it presupposes the life of obedience described in the preceding verses.

voices.html: Whatsoever we ask (ho ean aitōmen). Indefinite relative clause with modal an and the present active subjunctive. In form no limitations are placed here save that of complete fellowship with God, which means complete surrender of our will to God our Father. See Jesus' example in Mr 14:36.

These two things are connected, and none can really pray to God but those who with a pure heart fear and rightly worship him. The latter follows from the former. He who keeps his commandments is also one who pleases God.

Whatsoever we ask—In such a spirit, we receive of him, for he delights to bless the humble, upright, and sincere soul. Because we keep his commandments—Viz., by loving him and loving our neighbor. These are the great commandments both of the law and of the gospel.

And whatsoever we ask, we receive of him—if we are truly his children, and ask in a proper manner. Comp. Matt 7:7; Mark 11:24; John 14:12,13; 16:23. This implies asking in accordance with his will and in his Spirit.

23.synthesis: The single great commandment encompasses two inseparable elements: faith in Jesus Christ and love for one another. Robertson notes that hina introduces an appositive clause that constitutes the commandment itself—faith and love are not two separate commands but one command with two aspects. Calvin explains that faith must precede and generate love: "such is the discord between us and God, that we are kept off from access to him except we are united by love to one another. He not only commends faith, but adds love as its necessary companion." Clarke grounds the two parts: belief in Christ secures justification from what the Mosaic law could not pardon; love enables the Christian to fulfill the royal law. Wesley calls this "the greatest and most important command that ever issued from the throne of glory. If this be neglected, no other can be kept." Barnes observes that this commandment is the foundational principle on which all obedience rests. The aorist pisteusōmen suggests a decisive crisis act of faith that initiates the ongoing love.

voices.html: His commandment (hē entolē autou). That (hina)—subfinal use of hina in apposition with entolē and explanatory of it (Joh 15:12). The two points: believe in Christ (aorist or present subjunctive) and keep on loving one another.

The meaning is that such is the discord between us and God that we are kept off from access to him except we are united by love to one another. He not only commends faith, but adds love as its necessary companion—we must so begin with God as to make at the same time a transition to men.

And this is his commandment—All his commandments in one word. That we should believe and love—in the manner and degree which he hath taught. This is the greatest and most important command that ever issued from the throne of glory. If this be neglected, no other can be kept.

And this is his commandment—the leading, principal thing which he enjoins; the commandment which lies at the foundation of all true obedience: that we should believe on the name of his Son Jesus Christ and love one another.

24.synthesis: Keeping the commandments (faith and love, v23) is the mechanism of mutual indwelling: the believer abides in God, and God abides in the believer. Robertson traces the Spirit's role and marks this as the first explicit mention of the Spirit in the Epistle: "We abide in God and God abides in us through the Holy Spirit" (John 14:10, 17, 23; 17:21). Calvin notes that union with God cannot be "fruitless or without effect whenever it begins to exist," and the Spirit given is both proof and instrument of the union. Clarke is emphatic that Christians of old "knew by the Spirit, not by conjecture or inference"—the Spirit's witness was direct and experiential, not theoretical. Wesley describes the Spirit as the one who "witnesses with our spirits that we are his children, and brings forth his fruits of peace, love, holiness." Barnes calls the indwelling of the Spirit "another certain evidence that we are true Christians," connecting forward to 4:13 where the argument is continued.

voices.html: And he in him (kai autos en autō)—God abides in him. We abide in God and God abides in us through the Holy Spirit (Joh 14:10, 17, 23; 17:21). By the Spirit (ek tou pneumatos)—first mention of the Spirit in this Epistle. "Therefore let God be a home to thee, and be thou the home of God" (Bede).

He confirms that the union we have with God is evident when we entertain mutual love: not that our union begins thereby, but that it cannot be fruitless or without effect whenever it begins to exist. And he proves this by the gift of the Spirit.

Dwelleth in him—i.e. in God; and he—God, in him—the believer. And hereby we know—We know by the Spirit which he hath given us that we dwell in God, and God in us. It was not by conjecture or inference that Christians of old knew they were in God and God in them.

And he that keepeth his commandments—That thus believes and loves. Abideth in him, and God in him: and hereby we know that he abideth in us, by the Spirit which he hath given us—Which witnesses with our spirits that we are his children, and brings forth his fruits of peace, love, holiness.

1.synthesis: The command to test the spirits is as urgent as the command to love, because many false prophets have gone out into the world. Robertson reads the present imperative with mē as "stop believing"—implying that some in the community had already been carried along by spirits of error. Calvin explains that many were abusing the name of Christ to serve their own errors: "Some made a half profession of Christ; and when they obtained a place among his friends, they had more opportunity to injure his cause." Wesley roots the testing method in Scripture: "To the law and to the testimony! If any man speak not according to these, the spirit which actuates him is not of God." Clarke warns that false prophets are precisely those who profess divine commission—that profession is the very reason testing is necessary. Barnes insists that the Spirit of God can be discerned; it is not merely a matter of subjective impression. The tradition is united: credulity is not faith, and the test is external and objective.

voices.html: He returns to his former doctrine; for many abused the name of Christ for the purpose of serving their own errors. Some made a half profession of Christ; and when they obtained a place among his friends, they had more opportunity to injure his cause. Satan took occasion from this to spread his poison more widely.

Believe not every spirit (mē panti pneumati pisteuete). "Stop believing," as some were clearly carried away by the spirits of error rampant among them, both Docetic and Cerinthian Gnostics. Credulity means gullibility and some believers fall easy victims to the latest fads in spiritualistic humbug.

Believe not every spirit—Whereby any teacher is actuated. But try the spirits—By the rule which follows. We are to try all spirits by the written word: "To the law and to the testimony!" If any man speak not according to these, the spirit which actuates him is not of God.

The apostle had said that we could know that God dwells in us by the Spirit he has given us—but as it might be claimed that any spirit is from God, he now gives the test by which a true spirit may be known from a false one.

2.synthesis: The doctrinal test for the Spirit of God is confession that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh. Robertson explains the Greek construction: the perfect active participle en sarki elēluthota (predicate accusative) asserts not merely that Jesus became flesh at a moment in the past but that he has come and remains in that condition—the incarnation is permanent, not a temporary divine excursion. Calvin states that Christ "is the object at which faith aims and the stone at which all heretics stumble"—any teaching that relocates faith's center from the incarnate Christ is false prophecy. Wesley compresses the full weight: "his coming presupposes, contains, and draws after it the whole doctrine of Christ." Clarke observes that no one can sincerely call Jesus Lord in this sense except by the Holy Spirit (1 Cor 12:3). Barnes notes that this confession is essential to the entire Christian system; deny the true humanity and everything in Gethsemane dissolves into appearance. The test is doctrinal, objective, and non-negotiable.

voices.html: The correct text (perfect active participle predicate accusative, en sarki elēluthota) asserts not merely that Jesus became flesh but that he has come and remains in the flesh—the Incarnation is permanent. With the clamour of voices then and now this test is important.

He lays down a special mark by which they might more easily distinguish between true and false prophets. Christ is the object at which faith aims, so he is the stone at which all heretics stumble. As long then as we abide in Christ, we are safe from impostors.

Every spirit—Or teacher. Which confesseth—Both with heart and voice. Jesus Christ, who is come in the flesh, is of God. This his coming presupposes, contains, and draws after it the whole doctrine of Christ.

This doctrine is essential to the Christian system; and he who does not hold it cannot be regarded either as a Christian or recognised as a Christian teacher. If Jesus was not really man, then all that occurred in Gethsemane and on the cross was in appearance only, and atonement dissolves.

3.synthesis: Every spirit that does not confess Jesus Christ come in the flesh is not of God—and this is the spirit of antichrist, already present in the world. Robertson notes a significant variant in the Vulgate and several Church Fathers (Irenaeus, Tertullian, Augustine): luei ("separates" or "dissolves") rather than mē homologei ("confesseth not"), which Westcott takes as a paraphrase of Cerinthian teaching—the Gnostic dualism that separated the divine Christ from the human Jesus. Calvin treats this doctrine of antichrist as already well-known among the faithful: they had been warned, so they might "exercise more caution." Clarke adds the textual complexity: "is come in the flesh" is absent from several major early manuscripts and versions, suggesting the Johannine test was broader than a single phrase—any denial of the true person of Jesus falls under condemnation. Barnes draws the logical consequence: if Christ was not truly human, his suffering was not real, and the atonement is illusion.

voices.html: Confesseth not (mē homologei). The Vulgate (along with Irenaeus, Tertullian, Augustine) reads luei (separates Jesus), apparently an allusion to the Cerinthian Gnostics who separated the divine Christ from the human Jesus of Nazareth.

The Apostle added this to render more detestable the impostures which lead us away from Christ. We have already said that the doctrine respecting the kingdom of Antichrist was well known; so that the faithful had been warned as to the future scattering of the Church, in order that they might exercise more caution.

Every spirit—Every teacher—that confesseth not Jesus, is not of God—has not been inspired by God. The words en sarki elēluthota, "is come in the flesh," are wanting in several major manuscripts and versions, suggesting the broader scope of the test.

If Jesus was not really a man, then all that occurred in his life, in Gethsemane, and on the cross, was in appearance only, and was as unreal as the sufferings in a dream—and the atonement dissolves entirely.

4.synthesis: The believing community has already overcome the false prophets—the perfect tense nenikēkate indicates a settled, accomplished victory that remains in force. Robertson reads this as "calm confidence of final victory" (as in 2:13; John 16:33). The reason for the victory is not human wisdom or discernment but the greater power of the Spirit who abides in them: God is greater than Satan, the prince of this world (John 12:31; 14:30). Calvin offers the pastoral consolation: "It is no small consolation that they who dare to assail God in us have only the world to aid and help them." Clarke states the ontological contrast: "Greater is the Holy Spirit which is in you than the spirit which is in the world." Wesley reduces it to a maxim: "Ye have overcome these seducers, because greater is the Spirit of Christ that is in you than the spirit of antichrist that is in the world." Barnes notes that the faithful have triumphed not by their own resources but by the Spirit indwelling them.

voices.html: Have overcome them (nenikēkate autous). Perfect active indicative of nikaō, calm confidence of final victory as in 2:13; Joh 16:33. God is greater than Satan, "he that is in the world" (ho en tō kosmō), the prince of this world (Joh 12:31; 14:30).

It is no small consolation that they who dare to assail God in us have only the world to aid and help them. And by the world the Apostle means that portion of which Satan is the prince. Another consolation is added: that the world embraces through the false prophets that which it already loves.
Ye have overcome these seducers, because greater is the Spirit of Christ that is in you than the spirit of antichrist that is in the world.

Ye are of God—You are of his family; you have embraced his truth, and imbibed his Spirit. And have overcome them—Have triumphed over their arts and temptations; their endeavours to draw you into error and sin.

5.synthesis: False prophets are of the world; they speak from the world's perspective and the world welcomes them with approval. Robertson is precise and terse: the false prophets and the world are "in perfect unison"—they speak the same language, draw from the same well of worldly wisdom, and win a ready hearing from those who share their orientation. Calvin offers pastoral consolation: the world is all the false prophets have; their only supporters are those under Satan's dominion. Wesley observes that these teachers "speak from the same principle, wisdom, spirit; and of consequence the world heareth them with approbation." Barnes draws the diagnostic value: if a teacher's message is received indiscriminately by a worldly audience, that universal welcome is itself a ground for suspicion. The world recognizes its own. Consensus: the world-origin of false teaching and the world-endorsement of it are mutually confirming signs that identify the spirit of error.

voices.html: As of the world (ek tou kosmou). No "as" (hōs), but that is the idea, for their talk proceeds from the world and wins a ready hearing. The false prophets and the world are in perfect unison. As of the world so they speak.
It is no small consolation that they who dare to assail God in us have only the world to aid and help them. And by the world the Apostle means that portion of which Satan is the prince.
They—Those false prophets—are of the world. Therefore speak they of the world—From the same principle, wisdom, spirit; and of consequence the world heareth them—with approbation.

They are of the world. They belonged not to the church of God, but to the world. They had its spirit; they acted on its principles; they lived for it. Therefore speak they of the world—and the world heareth them.

6.synthesis: "We are of God"—and this gives the apostolic teaching its authority and provides the definitive criterion for identifying the spirit of truth. Robertson offers the striking image: "We are in tune with the Infinite God. Hence he who keeps on getting acquainted with God, growing in his knowledge of God, hears us." Calvin applies this primarily to faithful ministers of the gospel, grounded in the Spirit's confidence that they teach what they received from God: "the Apostle, through the confidence imparted by the Spirit, glories here that he and his fellow-ministers served God in sincerity." Clarke lays out the epistemological consequence: "He that knoweth God—who has a truly spiritual discernment, heareth us—acknowledges that our doctrine is from God." Barnes takes John to be referring to himself and the apostolic circle as the standard—those whose claims had been established beyond question were the criterion for discriminating spirit of truth from spirit of error. Receiving apostolic teaching is the mark of a God-knowing person.

voices.html: We (hēmeis). In sharp contrast with the false prophets and the world. We are in tune with the Infinite God. Hence he that knoweth God (ho ginōskōn ton theon)—the one who keeps on getting acquainted with God—hears us (akouei hēmōn).

Though this really applies to all the godly, yet it refers properly to the faithful ministers of the Gospel; for the Apostle, through the confidence imparted by the Spirit, glories here that he and his fellow-ministers served God in sincerity and derived from him whatever they taught.

We are of God—We, apostles, have the Spirit of God, and speak and teach by that Spirit. He that knoweth God—who has a truly spiritual discernment, heareth us—acknowledges that our doctrine is from God. Hereby know we the Spirit of truth and the spirit of error.

John here, doubtless, refers to himself and to those who taught the same doctrines which he did. He takes it for granted that those to whom he wrote would admit this, and argues from it as an indisputable truth.

7.synthesis: Love belongs to God and flows from God; therefore the one who loves has been born of God and is growing in genuine acquaintance with him. Robertson observes the double claim: even human love "comes from God, a reflection of something in the Divine nature itself" (Brooke)—and persistence in loving is proof that one has been begotten of God and is growing in knowledge of him. Calvin stresses the derivative character of human love: God's nature is to love men; love between brethren is not an independent human virtue but a participation in the divine nature. Clarke draws out the active implication: "He who loveth most has most of God in him." Wesley connects the Spirit directly: "It is by the Spirit that the love of God is shed abroad in our hearts"—love is pneumatological before it is ethical. Barnes notes that the transition from the doctrinal test (vv. 1–6) to the love test (vv. 7–21) is natural: both are tests of the same Spirit. Love is not a human achievement but a divine effusion.

voices.html: Of God (ek tou theou). Even human love comes from God, "a reflection of something in the Divine nature itself" (Brooke). Persistence in loving (present tense) is proof that one "has been begotten of God" and is acquainted with God. Mere claim to loving God accompanied by failure to love one's brother is hollow.

He returns to that exhortation which he enforces almost throughout the Epistle. When he commands love, he does not mean the love of man in general, but the love of brethren. He takes as granted a general truth: that God's nature is to love men.

Let us love one another—From the doctrine he has just been defending he draws this exhortation. It is by the Spirit that the love of God is shed abroad in our hearts. Every one that truly loveth God and his neighbour is born of God.

Beloved, let us love one another—And ever be ready to promote each other's welfare, both spiritual and temporal. For love is of God—And ever acts like him. He who loveth most has most of God in him; and he who loveth God will love his brother.

8.synthesis: "God is love"—four words that carry the greatest theological weight in the Epistle. Robertson is precise about the Greek: the predicate agapē is anarthrous (no article), making it a qualitative statement rather than an identity statement—John does not say that love is God (which would reverse subject and predicate and deify a human emotion), but that God is love, his essential nature expressed in all his acts. Calvin stresses the consequence: "there is no knowledge of God where there is no love"—the absence of love is proof of the absence of genuine divine acquaintance. Clarke draws out the relational implication: "He hates no thing that he has made. He cannot hate, because he is love." Wesley captures the fullness of what the sentence meant to John himself: "This little sentence brought St. John more sweetness, even in the time he was writing it, than the whole world can bring—his reigning attribute, the attribute that sheds an amiable glory on all his other perfections." Barnes: love is not merely one attribute among many but the essential character from which all other attributes operate.

voices.html: God is love (ho theos agapē estin). Anarthrous predicate, not hē agapē. John does not say that love is God, but only that God is love. The two are not interchangeable. God is the subject, love the predicate—his essential nature, not a reversible definition.

He also sets in opposition to this the contrary clause, that there is no knowledge of God where there is no love. And he takes as granted a general principle or truth, that God is love, that is, that his nature is to love men.

God is love—This little sentence brought St. John more sweetness, even in the time he was writing it, than the whole world can bring. This is his darling, his reigning attribute, the attribute that sheds an amiable glory on all his other perfections.
For God is love. He is not merely benevolent—love is not merely an attribute of God, one characteristic among many. He is love: his essential nature expressed in all his acts toward his creatures.

9.synthesis: The love of God has been manifested supremely and supremely concretely in the mission of his only-begotten Son. Robertson distinguishes the tenses: the perfect apestalken in this verse denotes the permanent mission of the Son (as again in v14), while the aorist apesteilem in verse 10 refers to the single historical event of sending—God's love was not a passing impulse but an enduring purpose. Clarke calls the incarnation "the fullest proof that God could give, or that man could receive, of his infinite love to the world." Calvin argues that though love is testified by many other things—creation, preservation, innumerable blessings—the mission of the Son is so preponderant that all other proofs "seem absorbed and lost in it." Barnes: "This was so prominent an instance of his love, that all the other manifestations of it seemed absorbed and lost in this." The tradition is united: the sending of the Son is the apex of divine love and the inescapable reference point for all subsequent reasoning about love's nature.

voices.html: Was manifested (ephanerōthē). First aorist passive of phaneroō. The Incarnation as in 3:5. Hath sent (apestalken). Perfect active indicative of apostellō, the permanent mission of the Son, though in verse 10 the aorist apesteilem occurs for the single event. His only-begotten Son into the world.

We have the love of God towards us testified also by many other proofs—why the world has been created, why we have been placed in it. But the Apostle meant by these words to teach us that God's love towards us has been so great that it is higher than all understanding.

In this was manifested the love of God—The mission of Jesus Christ was the fullest proof that God could give, or that man could receive, of his infinite love to the world. That we might live through him—The whole world was sentenced to death because of sin.

In this was manifested the love of God toward us—That is, in an eminent manner. This was so prominent an instance of his love, that all the other manifestations of it seemed absorbed and lost in this. Because that God sent his only-begotten Son into the world.

10.synthesis: Love is defined here by God's initiative, not ours: "Not that we loved God, but that He loved us." Robertson marks the sharp contrast ouch hoti... all' hoti (not that... but that), paralleled elsewhere in John 7:22 and 2 Corinthians 7:9—the logical structure of grace is always this asymmetry. Calvin insists that God's love "has been gratuitous from the first moment," citing Romans 5:8: we were enemies, not friends, when God acted. Clarke presses the same point: "We were enemies to God, and yet Christ died for our ungodly souls. So it was God's love, not our merit, that induced him to devise means." The word hilasmon (propitiation) carries the full weight of sacrificial language: the Son was sent not as a moral example but as the one who satisfies the divine justice that enables love to reach the guilty. Barnes: we were in no condition to deserve love, which makes the giving all the more astonishing. This is the order of love: God's love creates the conditions for our love, not the reverse.

voices.html: Not that (ouch hoti)—but that (all' hoti). Sharp contrast as in Joh 7:22; 2Co 7:9; Php 4:17. We loved (ēgapēsamen). First aorist active indicative. To be the propitiation (hilasmon). Merely predicate accusative in apposition with huion (Son). For the word see 2:2; Ro 3:25.

He amplifies God's love by another reason, that he gave us his own Son at the time when we were enemies, as Paul teaches us, in Romans 5:8; but he employs other words, that God, induced by no love of men, freely loved them.

Not that we loved God—And that he was thereby induced to give his Son to be a propitiation for our sins. No: we were enemies to God, and yet Christ died for our ungodly souls. So it was God's love, not our merit, that induced him to devise means.

Herein is love—In this great gift is the highest expression of love. Not that we loved God—Not that we were in such a state that we might suppose he would make such a sacrifice for us—just the opposite. This is the height of love: that God loved us before we were lovable.

11.synthesis: The Father's love creates the obligation to love one another. Calvin frames this as divine example pressed into ethical service: "God sets before us his own example that every one of us might labor to benefit his neighbor." Robertson notes houtōs emphasizing "both the manner and extent" of God's love (as in John 3:16), and the ongoing present infinitive agapain—"keep on loving." The logic is asymmetric grace turned into moral obligation: God loved those who were so inferior and so unworthy; we must love those who are on our level. Clarke insists that we must love "without suspending our love on his moral worth or his love to us"—the unconditional character of God's love to us shapes the required character of our love to one another. Barnes gives three reasons: (1) God is vastly higher than we, yet loved the inferior; (2) it is only in this way that we can show we have his Spirit; (3) it is love's very nature to generate love. The obligation is not sentimental but logical.

voices.html: Now the Almighty accommodates to his own purpose what he has just taught us respecting the love of God; for he exhorts us by God's example to brotherly love; as also Paul sets before us Christ, who offered himself to the Father a sacrifice of pleasant fragrance, that every one of us might labor to benefit his neighbor.

If God so loved us (ei houtōs ho theos ēgapēsen hēmas). Condition of first class with ei and the first aorist active indicative. As in Joh 3:16, so here houtōs emphasises the manifestation of God's love both in its manner and in its extent (Ro 8:32). Keep on loving (agapain).

If God so loved us—Without any reason or consideration on our part, and without any desert in us; we ought also, in like manner, to love one another, and not suspend our love to a fellow-creature, either on his moral worth or his love to us.

Beloved, if God so loved us, we ought also to love one another. (1) Because he is so much exalted above us, and if he has loved those who were so inferior and so unworthy; (2) because it is only in this way that we can show that we have his Spirit.

12.synthesis: No one has seen God at any time—yet if we love one another, God abides in us and his love is perfected in us. Robertson echoes John 1:18 virtually verbatim and observes the third-class condition—"if we keep on loving one another"—paired with the present-tense consequence: "God keeps on abiding in us. Else we cannot go on loving one another." Calvin extends the logic further: "the power of God dwells in those who entertain mutual love"—God's love, previously hidden from direct sight, becomes observable and operative within the community of love. Clarke: "We may feel him, though we cannot see him; and if we love one another he dwelleth in us, and his love is perfected in us." Wesley treats verse 12 as the thesis statement for the argument of verses 13–19, noting that the perfection of love (4:17–19) is the fuller treatment. Barnes reads the invisibility of God as the premise for a positive argument: the love-community is the visible theater in which God's otherwise invisible nature becomes observable and complete.

voices.html: No one hath beheld God at any time (theon oudeis pōpote tetheatai). Perfect middle indicative of theáomai—almost the very words of Joh 1:18. If we love one another (ean agapōmen allēlous)—third-class condition, "if we keep on loving." God abideth in us—else we cannot go on loving.

John extends the same truth farther, that the power of God dwells in those who entertain mutual love: not that we begin to know God only by loving one another, but that where love is not, there God does not appear in his power.

No man hath seen God at any time—The very words of this apostle in his gospel, Joh 1:18. We may feel him, though we cannot see him; and if we love one another he dwelleth in us, and his love is perfected in us—it has then its full accomplishment, having its full effect.

The statement seems to be made here to show in what way we may know that we have any true knowledge of God. The invisibility of God is the premise for a positive argument: the love-community is the visible theater in which God's love becomes observable and perfected.

13.synthesis: The indwelling Spirit is the Christian's assurance that he abides in God and God in him. Robertson marks the perfect tense dedōken here—"has given and still gives"—contrasted with the aorist edōken at 3:24: the permanent, ongoing gift that continuously grounds the believer's assurance. Calvin positions this as the experiential proof of what faith grasps: "God is so united to us by faith that we participate in him"—and the Spirit is both the means of that union and the testimony to it. Clarke cross-references 3:24, noting the same Spirit witness is in view. Barnes calls this "another, or an additional evidence"—the Spirit producing the fruit of love, joy, and peace (Gal 5:22–23) is itself the seal and proof of God's indwelling. Consensus: the Spirit-gift is both the instrument and the evidence of the divine union, connecting the abiding of verse 12 to the love and boldness of verses 17–18.

voices.html: Hereby know we (en toutō ginōskomen). The Christian's consciousness of the fact of God dwelling in him is due to the Spirit of God whom God has given (dedōken, perfect active indicative here, the permanent gift, though the aorist edōken in 3:24). This gift is proof of fellowship with God.

The design of the Apostle is to shew that God is so united to us by faith that we participate in him. He now explains the other part of the knowledge of God—that he communicates himself to us in his Son and offers himself to be enjoyed in him.
Hereby know we, etc.—See the note on 1Jn 3:24. The Spirit that he has given us gives this same testimony and grounds this same assurance.

Hereby know we that we dwell in him. Here is another, or an additional evidence of it. Because he hath given us of his Spirit—producing "love, joy, peace, long-suffering, gentleness, goodness, faith" (Galatians 5:22,23). It is the seal of the indwelling.

14.synthesis: The apostolic witness rests on eyewitness observation and direct testimony: "We have seen and testify that the Father has sent the Son as Savior of the world." Robertson distinguishes the perfect tethéametha—"we have beheld and the vision remains with us"—from the aorist of 1:1, emphasizing the lasting impression of what John personally witnessed. The title Sōtēr tou kosmou (Savior of the world) is striking—Robertson notes its appearance in John 4:42 on the lips of the Samaritans, suggesting its universal scope was recognized early. Calvin reads this as the grounding of the union described in verse 13: the apostles who bore eyewitness provided the testimony that grounds faith, and faith yields the Spirit-union with God. Clarke emphasizes the nature of the witness: "having the fullest conviction from his doctrine and miracles." Wesley ties this verse to the two foundations of assurance: the Spirit (v13) and the apostolic confession (v14). Barnes notes that John appeals to apostolic authority to establish the objective ground of the reader's faith.

voices.html: We have beheld (tethéametha). Perfect middle of theáomai as in verse 12. John is qualified to bear witness (marturoumen as in 1:2) as Jesus had charged the disciples to do (Ac 1:8). Savior of the world (sōtēra tou kosmou)—this very phrase in Joh 4:42 on the lips of the Samaritans.

He now explains the other part of the knowledge of God, that he communicates himself to us in his Son, and offers himself to be enjoyed in him. It hence follows that he is by faith received by us; and the apostles who bore eyewitness provided the testimony that grounds that faith.

And we have seen—Jesus Christ manifested in the flesh; see 1Jn 1:1—and do testify—bear witness, in consequence of having the fullest conviction, that the Father sent the Son to be the Saviour of the world.

And in consequence of this we have seen and testify that the Father sent the Son—These are the foundation and the criteria of our abiding in God and God in us, the communion of the Spirit and the confession of the Son.

15.synthesis: Confessing that Jesus is the Son of God effects a mutual indwelling: God abides in the confessor, and the confessor in God. Robertson emphasizes that genuine confession implies "surrender and obedience also, not mere lip service" (1 Cor 12:3; Rom 10:6–12)—it is proof, if genuine, of fellowship with God, not a formula that automatically secures it. Calvin distinguishes genuine confession from ordinary repetition: true confessors are those whose confession is accompanied by life; the Apostle "acknowledges none of those who ordinarily confess." Clarke notes the particular force of this confession in the anti-Docetic context: "false teachers denied the reality of the incarnation; but this confession implied also such a belief in Christ as put them in possession of his pardoning mercy and indwelling Spirit." Wesley emphasizes the public courage required: confessing "in the face of all opposition and danger" from a principle of loving faith, not merely academic assent. Barnes insists the confession must be "in the true sense, and from the heart."

voices.html: Whosoever shall confess (hos ean homologēsē)—indefinite relative clause, "whoever confesses." This confession of the deity of Jesus Christ implies surrender and obedience also, not mere lip service (cf. 1Co 12:3; Ro 10:6-12). Proof, if genuine, of fellowship with God.

He repeats the truth, that we are united to God by Christ, and that we cannot be connected with Christ except God abides in us. Though hypocrites may boast of faith, yet the apostle here acknowledges none of those who ordinarily confess, but only those whose confession is genuine.

Much stress is laid on this confession, because the false teachers denied the reality of the incarnation; but this confession implied also such a belief in Christ as put them in possession of his pardoning mercy and indwelling Spirit.
Whosoever shall, from a principle of loving faith, openly confess in the face of all opposition and danger that Jesus is the Son of God, God abideth in him.

16.synthesis: "We have known and believed the love that God has for us"—the perfect tense egnōkamen signals settled, accumulated knowledge, not a single moment of insight. Robertson explains the order: confession follows experimental knowledge (ginōskō) and confident trust (pisteuō); believers are "the sphere in which the love of God operates" (Westcott). Calvin reads knowing here as "knowing by believing"—a fusion of faith and knowledge that is not attained by reason alone but through genuine trust in the revealed love. The repetition of "God is love" from verse 8 is deliberate: Barnes notes that John "delights to dwell on such a glorious truth," and the dwelling in God and God in the believer is the necessary consequence of dwelling in love. Clarke: "God is the fountain and maintainer of love; he who dwells in love dwells in God, for God is love; and where such love is, there is God." The verse is both epistemological (how we know) and ontological (what union we have) simultaneously.

voices.html: We know (egnōkamen). Perfect active indicative, "we have come to know and still know." Confession (homologeō) follows experimental knowledge (ginōskō) and confident trust (pisteuō). Believers are the sphere (en hēmin) in which the love of God operates (Westcott).

It is the same as though he had said, "We have known by believing"; for such knowledge is not attained but by faith. Besides, though he meant here to accommodate the last sentence to his readers as a consolation, he yet bears testimony to himself.

God is love—see on 1Jn 4:8. He that dwelleth in love—he who is full of love to God and man is full of God, for God is love; and where such love is, there is God, for he is the fountain and maintainer of it.

And we have known and believed, etc. We all have assurance that God has loved us, and the fullest belief in the great fact of redemption by which he has manifested his love. It is not uncommon for John to repeat an important truth.

17.synthesis: Love is "perfected" in us in a specific way: so that we may have boldness (parrēsian) in the day of judgment. The analogy is compressed: "because as he is, so are we in this world." Robertson notes the interpretive question of whether en toutō governs the hina clause or the hoti clause, and follows Westcott in reading the hoti clause as giving the reason love is perfected with us—we are even now conformed to Christ in our relation to the world. Calvin identifies two movements: we partake of divine adoption by resembling God as children resemble their father; and this confidence before the judgment is the inestimable fruit of that adoption. Clarke presses the experiential language: "love is made perfect; when it thus fills the heart it has all its degrees." Wesley captures the eschatological force: "When all the stout-hearted shall tremble," those whose love has been perfected will stand with confidence, being as Christ is—all love—even in this world.

voices.html: Herein (en toutō). It is not clear whether the hina clause or the hoti clause is in apposition with en toutō. Westcott argues for the latter: the reason love is perfected with us is that we are even now like Christ in our relation to the world.

There are two clauses in this passage—that we are then partakers of divine adoption when we resemble God as children their father; and secondly, that this confidence is invaluable, for without it we must be most miserable. Then in the first place, he shews to what purpose God has in love embraced us.

Hereby—That is, by this communion with God. Is our love made perfect; that we may have boldness in the day of judgment—When all the stout-hearted shall tremble. Because as he—Christ. Is—All love. So are we—who are fathers in Christ, even in this world.

Herein is our love made perfect—By God dwelling in us, and we in him. The love is made perfect; when it thus fills the heart it has all its degrees; it is perfect love—a love that produces boldness in the day of judgment.

17.key_tension: Calvin and Robertson stress boldness in judgment as the fruit of adoption-resemblance—a judicial confidence grounded in our relation to God; Wesley and Clarke press toward love's completeness in degree as an experiential state achievable in this life prior to the day of judgment.

18.synthesis: Perfect love casts out fear—not the reverent awe of a son before a father, but the servile dread of a slave awaiting punishment. Robertson distinguishes precisely: the phobos driven out is "like a bond-slave (Ro 8:15), not the reverence of a son (Heb 5:7f.) nor the obedience to a father (1Pe 1:17)"—it is the torment-producing dread that is the opposite of boldness. Calvin explains the mechanism: "there is nothing more miserable than to be harassed by continuous anxiety... God delivers us from misery and anguish by the remedy of his own love towards us." Clarke observes the devotional consequence: "The man who feels that he loves God with all his heart can never dread him as his Judge. As he is now made a partaker of his Spirit, and carries a sense of the Divine approbation in his conscience, he has nothing of that fear that produces terror." Wesley draws a developmental scale: natural man has neither fear nor love; the awakened, fear without love; the babe in Christ, love and fear; the father in Christ, love without slavish fear. Barnes: if a man had perfect love, there would be literally nothing left to dread.

voices.html: Fear (phobos). Like a bond-slave (Ro 8:15), not the reverence of a son (Heb 5:7f.) nor the obedience to a father (1Pe 1:17). This kind of dread is the opposite of parrēsia (boldness). Perfect love (hē teleia agapē). Casteth out fear (exō ballei ton phobon)—drives fear out.

He now commends the excellency of this blessing by stating the contrary effect, for he says that we are continually tormented until God delivers us from misery and anguish by the remedy of his own love towards us. The meaning is that as there is nothing more miserable than to be harassed by continuous anxiety, God has provided the remedy of his love.

There is no fear in love—No slavish fear can be where love reigns. A natural man has neither fear nor love; one that is awakened, fear without love; a babe in Christ, love and fear; a father in Christ, love without slavish fear. Because such fear hath torment—and so is inconsistent with the happiness of love.

The man who feels that he loves God with all his heart can never dread him as his Judge. As he is now made a partaker of his Spirit, and carries a sense of the Divine approbation in his conscience, he has nothing of that fear that produces terror or brings torment.

19.synthesis: "We love him because he first loved us"—the entire dynamic of Christian love is articulated in one compressed sentence. Robertson notes autos prōtos—nominative, not prōton—emphasizing that the priority belongs to the subject (God), not merely to the sequence of events: God as the originating agent loved first, before any movement on our side (John 3:16). Calvin reads verse 19 as deliberately repeating verse 16 to seal the point: "God has anticipated us by his free love, we ought to return to render love to him." Clarke enumerates the grounds of our love in threefold movement: we love because (1) we find that he has loved us; (2) we love from a sense of obligation and gratitude; (3) we love from the influence of his own love shed abroad in our hearts by the Spirit (Rom 5:5). Wesley offers the compression: "This is the sum of all religion, the genuine model of Christianity. None can say more: why should any one say less?" Barnes notes that whether God's prior love is the ground or the occasion of ours, the result is the same: the priority belongs entirely to God.

voices.html: He first (autos prōtos). Note prōtos (nominative), not prōton, as in Joh 20:4, 8. God loved us before we loved him (Joh 3:16). Our love is in response to his love for us. There is no object expressed here.
We love him, because he first loved us—This is the sum of all religion, the genuine model of Christianity. None can say more: why should any one say less, or less intelligibly?

We love him because he first loved us—This is the foundation of our love to God. (1) We love him because we find he has loved us. (2) We love him from a sense of obligation and gratitude. (3) We love him from the influence of his own love—shed abroad in our hearts by the Spirit.

The verb may be either indicative or imperative; but the former is the more suitable here, for the Apostle repeats the preceding sentence, that as God has anticipated us by his free love, we ought to return to render love to him.

20.synthesis: Claiming to love God while hating a visible brother is a self-refuting lie. Robertson is blunt: "A liar—blunt and to the point as in 1:10"—using the same pseustēs that earlier exposed false claims about sin. Calvin turns the apparent lesser-to-greater argument on its head: it might seem harder to love an invisible God than a visible brother, but if we cannot love the brother right in front of us—triggering every natural impulse of sympathy and compassion—we certainly cannot love the God we have never seen. Wesley makes the epistemological point quietly: the brother is "daily presented to his senses, to raise his esteem and move his kindness or compassion toward him." Clarke suggests the audience included Jewish converts who professed love to God while harboring contempt for Gentile brethren—a first-century specific application of the universal principle. Barnes states it cleanly: whatever one's profession, visible hatred of a neighbor disproves all claims to love the invisible God.

voices.html: A liar (pseustēs). Blunt and to the point as in 1:10. And hateth (kai misei)—continuation of the condition with ean and the present active subjunctive, "and keep on hating." See 2:9; 3:15 for use of miseō (hate) with adelphos (brother).

But this love cannot exist, except it generates brotherly love. Hence he says, that they are liars who boast that they love God, when they hate their brethren. If we love not our brethren whom we see, much less can we love God whom we see not.
Whom he hath seen—Who is daily presented to his senses, to raise his esteem and move his kindness or compassion toward him.
If a man say, I love God, and hateth his brother—He is a liar. It is not necessary to say that he is conscious of this; the point is that it is impossible for a man to love God and hate his brother.

21.synthesis: The final verse closes the circle: the commandment from God himself is that he who loves God must also love his brother. Robertson notes the hina sub-final clause in apposition with entolēn (commandment), exactly as in John 13:34 and 15:13—the two loves are constitutively linked in a single command, not two separate obligations that happen to coexist. Calvin uses this as the transition to chapter 5: "We must therefore so begin with God, as that there may be at the same time a transition made to men." Wesley turns the verse against religious bigotry: "A bigot only loves those who embrace his opinions and receive his mode of worship. The commandment demands love of every person who bears the image of God, purely because he is the child of God." Barnes states the symmetry: the command to love a brother "is as obligatory as that to love God. If one is obeyed, the other ought to be also." Clarke makes the theological synthesis: "The love of God and the love of man can never be separated; he who loves God will love his brother."

voices.html: That (hina)—sub-final object clause in apposition with entolēn as in Joh 13:34; 15:13. From him (ap' autou)—either God or Christ. See Mr 12:29-31 for this old commandment (2:7f.).

This is a stronger argument, drawn from the authority and doctrine of Christ; for he not only gave a commandment respecting the love of God, but bade us also to love our brethren. We must therefore so begin with God, as that there may be at the same time a transition made to men.

And this commandment have we from him—Both God and Christ. That he who loveth God love his brother—Every one, whatever his opinions or mode of worship be, purely because he is the child and bears the image of God. Bigotry is properly the want of this pure and universal love.

And this commandment have we from him—That is, the command to love a brother is as obligatory as that to love God. If one is obeyed, the other ought to be also; and he can never have evidence that he is a true Christian, unless he manifests love to his brethren.
